coredata.dc:title: Hollow luminescent carbon dots for drug delivery
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qinlong
Huang, Xiaoxiao
Long, Yijuan
Wang, Xiliang
Zhang, Haijie
Zhu, Rui
Liang, Liping
Teng, Ping
Zheng, Huzhi
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are luminescent nanomaterials with unique properties that show great potential in many applications. Herein, hollow CDs (HCDs) are prepared from bovine serum albumin by solvothermal reaction. The obtained HCDs are ca. 6.8nm in diameter and have a quantum yield of 7%. Their bright photoluminescence means they can be used for cellular imaging. Structure and composition analyses indicate that the HCDs possess a hollow structure with a pore size of ca. 2nm. The HCDs are used as a delivery system for doxorubicin (DOX). The DOX-HCD drug delivery system exhibits pH-controlled release, and is rapidly taken up by cells. Because of their specific nanostructure and photoluminescence properties, the multifunctional HCDs prepared here show potential for application in both cell imaging and cancer therapy.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Loading and pH-dependent release of DOX
Cytotoxicity of HCDs and the anti-cancer cell activity of DOX-HCDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation Project of CQ CSTC

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Scientific Program-Nanoscience and Nanotechnology (No. 2011CB933600), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21175110), and the Natural Science Foundation Project of CQ CSTC (CSTC, 2010BB4004). We thank Prof. Huang Chengzhi, Prof. Huang Yuming, Dr. Liu Changhua and Dr. Tang Qian (Southwest University) for their help with experimental measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM image and magnification (inset) of the hollow structure of HCDs. (B) Size distribution of HCDs.
(A) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and (B) pore size distribution of HCDs.
(A) Normalized UV–Vis absorption spectra of HCDs (solid line) and BSA (dashed line). (B) Normalized photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of HCDs (solid lines) and BSA (dashed lines).
Graphical illustration of the synthesis of luminescent HCDs and the loading of DOX.

Fluorescence images of A549 cells incubated with DOX-HCDs for 24h (HCDs 33.3μgmL−1, DOX 2μgmL−1) observed under (A) bright-field, (B) excitation of GFP channel at 475nm, (C) observed with the Cy 3 channel, and (D) merged images.
Schematic illustration of the intracellular release of DOX from the DOX-HCD drug delivery system.

head.ce:title: Hollow luminescent carbon dots for drug delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analysis, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analysis
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are luminescent nanomaterials with unique properties that show great potential in many applications. Herein, hollow CDs (HCDs) are prepared from bovine serum albumin by solvothermal reaction. The obtained HCDs are ca. 6.8nm in diameter and have a quantum yield of 7%. Their bright photoluminescence means they can be used for cellular imaging. Structure and composition analyses indicate that the HCDs possess a hollow structure with a pore size of ca. 2nm. The HCDs are used as a delivery system for doxorubicin (DOX). The DOX-HCD drug delivery system exhibits pH-controlled release, and is rapidly taken up by cells. Because of their specific nanostructure and photoluminescence properties, the multifunctional HCDs prepared here show potential for application in both cell imaging and cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent carbon dots (CDs) are attractive carbon nanomaterials that show advantages for application in photocatalysis [1], light-emitting devices [2] and sensing [3]. CDs are nanoscale (<10nm) spherical carbon particles that possess a variety of unique properties such as high photostability against photobleaching and blinking [4], tunable emission spectra [4], low molecular weight [4,5], excellent biocompatibility [4] and low toxicity [6,7]. These properties meet the requirements of optical imaging agents; hence CDs have considerable advantageous over semiconductor quantum dots as well as conventional organic dyes in biological labeling and bioimaging [8–10]. Several groups have applied luminescent CDs in bioimaging [7]. For example, Sun and co-workers used passivated CDs in in vitro cell imaging with both one- and two-photon excitations [4,8,9]. They also successfully used CDs as in vivo optical imaging agents [10]. Ray et al. used CDs in cell imaging and demonstrated their ability to enter cells without any further functionalization [11]. These promising findings inspire further investigation of luminescent CDs in bioimaging.

Because of desirable chemical and physical properties such as high surface areas and excellent flow performances, hollow nanomaterials are expected to be useful in drug delivery systems [12], and as catalyst carriers [13] and absorbents [14]. Drug delivery systems that display controlled drug release [12,15] and targeting [15,16] are needed to meet the demands of modern diagnosis and therapy, so nanomaterials with hollow structures deserve more attention in this regard. Nanoparticles with properties suitable for multi-imaging have received significant attention and can be used in multifunctional composite systems. Extensive effort has been devoted to designing nanocarriers that combine cell targeting with efficient in vivo imaging, drug storage, controlled drug release capabilities [15,17], detection and isolation of cancer cells [18].

As the need for fluorescent bioimaging agents with specific properties increases, CD synthetic approaches and studies on their function-related properties have increased [7]. The combination of luminescent CDs and hollow nanostructures may provide multifunctional platforms for applications in optics, drug delivery and catalysis. Such combination broadens the “dot” concept and improves the CD compatibility with other species to realize diverse functional materials [19]. In this work, hollow CDs (HCDs) with bright photoluminescence were prepared from bovine serum albumin (BSA) by solvothermal treatment under mild temperature conditions. HCDs can serve as a multifunctional vehicle for drug loading and release because of their hollow structure and excellent photoluminescence. Doxorubicin (DOX) with inherent fluorescence was selected as a model anticancer drug to form a dual emission delivery system. Cell viability and cellular uptake results suggest that the HCDs exhibit low toxicity and act as a potential platform for bioapplications. The HCD-based drug delivery system possesses a number of advantages including rapid cellular uptake, pH-controlled drug release, biocompatibility and excitation-dependent photoluminescence.

Freeze-dried BSA was obtained from Genview (Japan). Doxorubicin hydrochloride was purchased from Melonepharma (Dalian, China). All other reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2MΩ) was prepared with a Milli-Q system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) and was used in all experiments. Hoechst 33342 and DiD were purchased from Invitrogen (USA). Lyso-Tracker Red was obtained from Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). Fluorescein-5-isothiocyanate (FITC) was purchased from Molecular Probes (USA).

BSA (10mg) was dissolved in ultrapure water (5mL). Ethanol (5mL) was added and then the mixture was sonicated until a homogeneous solution formed. The solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with a capacity of 30mL. The autoclave was heated at 180°C for 12h and then cooled to room temperature. The resultant light brown solution was separated by centrifugation at 12,000rpm for 30min. The supernatant was then loaded into dialysis bags (molecular weight cutoff=3500Da, Shanghai Green Bird Science & Technology Development Co., China) for dialysis against ultrapure water for two days to purify the HCDs.

An aqueous solution of HCDs (5μL, about 1×10−5 gmL−1) was added onto a 400-mesh carbon-coated copper grid (SPI, USA) and then dried under vacuum at 40°C. The size of the HCDs was then determined by field emission transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI, USA). The size distribution was determined by measuring the diameters of 150 particles. Scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) images were obtained in STEM mode.

A sample for atomic force microscopy (AFM) was prepared by adding an aqueous solution of HCDs (5μL, about 1×10−5 gmL−1) onto a freshly cleaved mica surface, which was then dried under vacuum. AFM images were taken using a Nanoscope Quadrex AFM (Veeco, USA). All aqueous solutions were air-equilibrated and measured without further treatment.

Absorption and photoluminescence spectra were recorded using a UV-2450-PC spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and F-4500 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan), respectively. Luminescence lifetimes were measured using a fluorescence spectrometer (Fluorolog-3, Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., France).

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with an infrared spectrophotometer (TENSOR 27, Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were collected using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB250, Thermo, UK). Raman spectra were recorded on a Raman spectrometer (RFS-100/s FT, Bruker, Germany). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a thermal analyzer (STA-449C, Netzsch, Germany) under a flow of N2 gas at a heating rate of 10°Cmin−1. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on an X-ray diffractometer (XD-3, Persee, China) using Cu Kα radiation. N2 adsorption/desorption measurements were performed on an automated physisorption and chemisorption analyzer (Autosorb-1, Quantachrome, USA). Zeta potential and hydrodynamic size were measured using a zetasizer (Nano ZS90, Malvern Instruments, UK).

Loading of DOX into HCDs was carried out simply by adding DOX (0.1mgmL−1) to an aqueous solution of HCDs in the dark and stirring the solution overnight. The mixture was dialyzed against ultrapure water in the dark to remove unbound DOX and then filtered to remove any precipitates. The DOX loading ratio was calculated based on the absorbance of DOX at 490nm after subtracting the absorbance of HCDs at same wavelength. The resultant DOX-HCD solution was stored at 4°C.

DOX-HCD solution (10mL) was dialyzed in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) (500mL) at pH of 7.4 and 5.0. The release system was kept in the dark and stirred slowly. To evaluate the amount of released DOX, the dialysis bags were taken out at various intervals and the concentration of DOX released from HCDs was quantified using a UV-2450 spectrophotometer.

A549 cells (adenocarcinomic human alveolar basal epithelial cells), Hela cells (human cervical cancer cells) and MRC-5 cells (normal human fetal lung fibroblast cells) were cultured and maintained in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) (Gibco, USA). HCCLM9 cells (hepatocellular carcinoma cells) were cultured and maintained in 1640 medium (Gibco, USA). Both media were supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) (Gibco, USA), penicillin G (100UmL−1), streptomycin sulfate (100μgmL−1), sodium pyruvate (110mgL−1), l-glutamine and pyridoxine hydrochloride. Cells were maintained at 37°C in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2.

A549 cells were seeded at a density of 2.0×105 cells per dish onto a 35-mm culture dish with a cover glass on the bottom. The A549 cell monolayer was split onto glass cover slips in 24-well microplates and incubated at 37°C for 1day. The DOX-HCD solution was diluted to a final concentration of 33.3μgmL−1 by DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS. The cells were incubated with DOX-HCDs for 24h and the A549 cell monolayer was then washed three times with 1× PBS and fixed on a slide with 3% paraformaldehyde for 30min. Cellular images were obtained with a rotary confocal fluorescence microscope (Olympus, Japan), including an IX2-DSU confocal scanning system, an IX-81 inverted fluorescence microscope with a 40× objective (NA=0.95) and an electron multiplying charge coupled device (EMCCD; Rolera-MGi). The control software used was In Vivo 3.2/3D Analyzer Suite 6.2 (Media Cybernetics Inc., USA). Image analysis and processing were performed with Image-Pro Plus Version 6.3 (Media Cybernetics Inc., USA).

For fluorescence colocalization imaging, A549 cells were harvested by detachment using trypsin solution, and then re-suspended in fresh medium. The cells were then plated in 35-mm glass-bottom culture dishes (NEST Science Co., Ltd., China) and grown to 50% confluence prior the addition of DOX or DOX-HCDs. After incubation for a certain time, cells were stained with Hoechst (cellular nucleus dye), DiD (cellular membrane dye) or Lyso-Tracker Red (lysosome dye). The cells were then washed several times with pre-chilled PBS (pH=7.4) to remove residual particles. Finally, the culture dishes were visualized under a spinning-disk confocal microscope (Revolution XD, Andor, UK) equipped with a microscope (IX 81, Olympus), a disk-type confocal unit (CSU 22, Nipkow, Yokogawa, Japan), a CO2 online culture system (INUBG2-PI, Tokai HIT, Japan) and a single photon detector EMCCD (iXon DV885K, Andor, UK). Excitation and emission filters were set as follows: DOX or DOX-HCDs, 488nm excitation, emission BP 617/73nm; Hoechst, 405nm excitation, emission BP 447/60nm; DiD, 640nm excitation, emission BP 685/40nm; Lyso-Tracker Red, 561nm excitation, emission BP 605/20nm. Fluorescent emission was collected using a 40× oil immersion objective lens (NA=1.0) or 100× oil immersion objective lens (NA=1.4). For simultaneous three-color imaging, the fluorescence was imaged alternately onto the EMCCD through three channels. Each frame was processed with a Gauss filter to remove background noise. The colocalization of HCDs, DOX or DOX-HCDs with cellular structures was identified with Imaging-Pro-Plus software and confirmed by eye. The criteria for colocalization were that the objects remained together and had at least partial overlap.

To study the biocompatibility of HCDs, A549 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate with a density of about 2×104 cells per well and cultured to 80% confluence in 5% CO2 at 37°C for 24h. HCDs were then added and the cells were incubated in 5% CO2 at 37°C for a further 24 or 48h. The final concentrations of HCDs were 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200 and 400μgmL−1. The cell viability was determined using a Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) assay based on 2-(2-methoxy-4-nitrophenyl)-3-(4-nitrophenyl)-5-(2,4-disulfophenyl)-2H-tetrazolium (WST-8). Finally, cellular viability was determined at 450nm by a microplate reader (Multiskan Mk3, Thermo Fisher Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China).

To analyze the in vitro cytotoxicity of free DOX and DOX-HCDs, A549, Hela, HCCLM9 and MCG-5 cells were seeded in 96-well plates with a density of about 2×104 cells per well and then cultured to 80% confluence in 5% CO2 at 37°C for 24h. DOX or DOX-HCDs were added to the media and the cells were incubated in 5% CO2 at 37°C for a further 24 or 48h. The final concentrations of free DOX and DOX-HCDs were 0.5, 1, 2 and 3.5μgmL−1, and the corresponding final concentrations of HCDs were 10, 20, 40 and 70μgmL−1. Cell viability was determined using the CCK-8 assay. Statistical analysis of experimental data was performed using the Student’s t-test. A p-value of less than 0.01 or 0.05 was considered statistically significant. Each data point was represented as the mean±standard deviation (SD) of three independent experiments (n =3, where n indicates the number of wells in a plate for each set of experimental conditions).

Quantum yields (QYs) were measured according to “A Guide to Recording Fluorescence QYs” by Jobin Yvon Horiba Ltd. 1
http://www.jobinyvon.co.uk/ukdivisions/Fluorescence/plqy.htm.
1 Quinine sulfate in a 0.1M H2SO4 aqueous solution of H2SO4 (QY=0.54) was selected as a reference. The QY of HCDs was calculated by comparing integrated photoluminescence intensity (excited at 360nm) and absorbance (less than 0.1 at the excitation wavelength) of each sample with those of quinine sulfate. The slope method was used to calculate the QY of HCDs using the equation:
QY
u
=
QY
s
(
m
u
/
m
s
) (
n
u
/
n
s
)
2
,
where m is the slope of a curve, and n is the refractive index (1.33 for H2O and a 0.1M aqueous solution of H2SO4). The subscript “s” refers to the reference compound and “u” refers to an unknown (the sample). For these aqueous solutions, n u/n s =1. Series of concentrations of both the reference and HCD samples were measured to obtain the slopes of the curves.

The size of HCDs was characterized using TEM, AFM and dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements. TEM images showed that HCDs were well dispersed with a narrow size distribution (average diameter=6.8±1.3nm for 150 measured particles) (Fig. 1 , Fig. S1). Correspondingly, AFM images revealed that HCDs possessed a narrow size distribution with an average height of ca. 8nm (Fig. S2), which agrees with the above TEM results. DLS measurements showed that the average diameter of HCDs was 13nm (Fig. S3). This value is also consistent with the size determined from TEM images because DLS measures the overall hydrodynamic size of particles.

The hollow structure of the HCDs was clearly observed in high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig. 1A). To further confirm the HCDs contained hollow interiors, N2 adsorption measurements were performed, as illustrated in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2A shows an ink-bottle-type IV N2 adsorption isotherm, implying the HCDs possess a hollow structure. This observation is consistent with the HRTEM images shown in Fig. 1A. Calculations based on the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda model indicated the HCDs were hollow with a surface area of 16.4m2 g−1, pore volume of 1.73×10−2 cm3 g−1 and an average simulated pore diameter of 2.2nm [20]. A broad pore size distribution was observed, as presented in Fig. 2B.

Other structural properties of the HCDs were also investigated by XRD and Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S4 A and B, respectively). A broad, weak peak centered at 18.9° (002), was observed, which indicated an amorphous carbon structure. A Raman spectrum of the HCDs displayed two prominent peaks at 1607 and 1319cm−1 that correspond to G and D bands, respectively. The D band is attributed to a disordered crystalline structure with sp2 configuration, while the G band is related to ideal single-crystal graphite. The higher intensity of the D band suggested the HCDs possess an imperfect graphite structure with defect-rich regions. In addition to structural analysis of HCDs, XPS was used to obtain compositional information (Fig. S5 and Table S1). A high content of carbon indicated that carbonization had occurred. High resolution XPS spectra revealed that the main types of carbon on the surface of HCDs were sp2 CC (284.5eV) and oxygen/nitrogen-bonded carbon. The presence of C–O (531.9eV) and CO (287.3 and 534.2eV) bonds indicated that the surfaces of the HCDs were rich in oxygen-containing groups. FT-IR spectroscopy was also used to characterize the surface functional groups on the HCDs (Fig. S4C). A broad absorption peak at 3400cm−1 was attributed to C–OH vibrational stretching, implying the presence of numerous hydroxyl groups. Other peaks consistent with oxygen-containing groups, including a CO vibration (1676cm−1), symmetric carboxylate stretch (1386cm−1) and epoxy group vibration (1108cm−1) were also observed. These abundant functional groups not only give the HCDs a negative charge (zeta potential ζ =−16.7mV), but also contribute to their solubility and stability. Other than BSA, solvothermal carbonization of lysozyme can also obtain HCDs (Fig. S6).

The thermal stability of HCDs was studied by TGA under a N2 atmosphere (Fig. S4D). HCDs exhibited good thermostability up to 200°C with a weight loss of only about 7% in this range because HCDs were prepared at 180°C. The weight loss that occurred in the range of 200–400°C was about 50%, which inferred the complete carbonization of HCDs and the release of pyrogas (such as CO and CO2). From 400 to 680°C, the TGA curve leveled off, which indicated that the incompletely carbonized fraction of the HCDs transformed to carbon materials with a carbon yield of nearly 30%.

The size of products formed during solvothermal treatment was also monitored. When dispersed in solution by ultrasonication, BSA monomers tend to form aggregates of uniform size (ca. 453nm, Fig. S7). After solvothermal treatment for 4h, BSA was denatured and formed non-uniform aggregates with sizes ranging from ca. 10 to 80nm (Fig. S8). Smaller aggregates (ca. 23.3nm) of denatured BSA protein were observed after 6h of solvothermal treatment (Fig. S9). These aggregates separate into carbon nanoparticles under the extreme solvothermal conditions. After solvothermal treatment for 8h, nanoparticles formed with an average diameter of ca. 15.8±2.4nm for 150 measured particles (Fig. S10). STEM images confirmed that these particles were not hollow (Fig. S10). At this time, carbonization only existed on the surface of small aggregates and carbonization was not complete. The incompletely carbonized interior of the nanoparticles may prevent the shell from sinking or breaking. Finally, after 12h incubation, the incompletely carbonized interior was carbonized and HCDs appeared (Fig. 1A). The change of product size during solvothermal treatment is presented in Fig. S11.

HCDs displayed a broad absorption peak centered at 230nm, which differs from the typical absorption peak of proteins (ca. 280nm) (Fig. 3 A). This new absorption peak was ascribed to the π–π∗ transition of aromatic domains in the carbonized shell of the HCDs [21]. The excitation and emission maxima of HCDs are located at 360 and 440nm, respectively (Fig. 3B). The full width at half maximum of the HCDs was about 120nm. Similar to other CDs [4], the emission of HCDs was excitation dependent (Fig. S12). The lifetime of the HCDs was 5.85ns (Fig. S13), and their QY was 7.0±0.61% (P =0.90, average of 5 batches, Table S2). HCDs remain stable in aqueous solution for at least 3months at room temperature without coagulation or a decrease in photoluminescence intensity. Meanwhile, almost no bleaching was observed after continuous irradiation with UV light for 1h, suggesting the emission was quite stable (Fig. S14).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading and pH-dependent release of DOX
ce:section.ce:para: The hollow structure and luminescent properties of HCDs make them suitable for use as drug carriers. An anticancer drug, DOX, was selected as a model to test the HCDs as a drug delivery system. DOX was directly mixed with HCD in solution (Fig. 4 ), where it was physisorbed through interactions such as π–π stacking, hydrophobic and van der Waals interactions; this behavior is similar to other carbon nanomaterials [22–24]. Unbound DOX was removed by dialysis and filtration. A visible peak at around 490nm (the characteristic absorption wavelength of DOX) suggested the formation of DOX-HCDs (Fig. S15A). The surface zeta potential also changed from −16.7 to −8.0mV after DOX loading. Both HCDs and DOX maintained their own photoluminescence with a slight decrease in intensity to form a dual emission system (Fig. S15B). The loading ratio of DOX was calculated to be 6.0wt.% (average of 5 batches). DOX-HCD solutions were stable for several weeks without aggregation and emitted red fluorescence under UV irradiation (Fig. 4).

An important property of drug delivery systems is the controlled release of drugs from the carrier. The pH-controlled release of DOX by HCDs was investigated in vitro by comparing the release rates at pH 7.4, which mimics the extracellular environment, and at 5.0, which mimics the acidic conditions of intracellular lysosomes. Only about 4% of DOX was released at pH 7.4, while ca. 70% was released at pH 5.0 (Fig. S16). Such pH-controlled release is attributed to an increase in the solubility of DOX in water under acidic conditions [22–24].

Another important property of drug delivery systems is ready internalization by cells. The internalization of DOX-HCDs was observed by fluorescence microscopy (Fig. 5 ). Using the excitation-dependent emission of HCDs, green emission was observed from HCDs under blue illumination. After 24h incubation, HCDs were found throughout the cell cytoplasm (Fig. 5B), surrounding nuclei in particular. This observation confirmed that HCDs could be internalized by A549 cells and were mainly localized in the cytoplasm but could not enter the nucleus. However, bright red fluorescence from DOX was observed in cell nuclei, indicating that DOX was almost completely released from HCDs and entered cell nuclei (Fig. 5C and D), in agreement with other reports [25]. The HCDs exhibited excellent photostability, which suggests that they may also be used for cellular imaging (Fig. 5B).

Cellular uptake and intracellular trafficking of DOX-HCDs were studied in detail by fluorescence colocalization imaging of cells. A549 cells were incubated with DOX-HCDs for certain durations at 37°C and then stained with Hoechst and DiD to identify nuclei and cell membranes. DOX-HCDs (red) clearly colocalized with DiD (green) after 30min incubation (Fig. S17). An orthogonal slice view showed that the colocalized regions exhibited yellow emission in three dimensions, which revealed the positions of the vesicles (Fig. S17E). These findings confirmed that DOX-HCDs entered cells by endocytosis and formed vesicles. Vesicles formed by invagination of the cell membrane during endocytosis traveled into the cytosol and fused with lysosomes [26].

To further understand the intracellular trafficking of DOX-HCDs, HCDs were labeled with FITC to form FITC-HCDs, incubated with A549 cells for certain times at 37°C, and then A549 cells were stained with Lyso-Tracker Red. The emission from HCDs was not considered here because of the relatively weak photoluminescence of bare HCDs compared with that of the fluorescent dye. Colocalization of FITC-HCDs with lysosomes was confirmed by the observation of yellow emission caused by the overlap of green (FITC-HCDs) and red (Lyso-Tracker Red) fluorescence in the orthogonal slice view (Figs. S18, S19). This indicates that HCDs were transported into the lysosomes after endocytosis. FITC-HCDs mainly accumulated in the cytoplasm and did not penetrate the nuclear membrane.

After incubation of DOX-HCDs with A549 cells for 1.5h, red fluorescence from DOX was found in the nuclei. This emission colocalized with the blue fluorescence from Hoechst very well, confirming that DOX is released by HCDs and enters the cell nuclei (Fig. S20).

Overall, cellular uptake and intracellular trafficking mechanisms of DOX-HCDs were clearly demonstrated. First, the DOX-HCDs entered the cells by endocytosis and formed vesicles (Fig. S17). Second, the DOX-HCD vesicles were transported into the lysosomes (Figs. S18, S19). Finally, in the acidic environment of the lysosomes, the protonated DOX was released and then entered the nuclei (Fig. 6 and Fig. S20).

A549 cells were incubated with DOX-HCDs or free DOX for different periods and then the cells were observed by fluorescence microscopy. Intense red fluorescence from DOX-HCDs was observed in the cells but no obvious red fluorescence from free DOX was seen in the cells after incubation for 1h, which suggests the DOX-HCDs entered the cells faster than free DOX (Fig. S21). This phenomenon may be caused by the different uptake mechanisms of DOX-HCDs (endocytosis) and free DOX (flip-flop mechanism [27]).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of HCDs and the anti-cancer cell activity of DOX-HCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the possible side effects of this drug delivery system on cells, the toxicity of HCDs and anti-cancer cell activity of DOX-HCDs were investigated. A549 cells were incubated with a series of bare HCDs of different concentration for 24 and 48h. The relative cellular viabilities after these periods were determined by CCK-8 assay [28]. Almost no growth inhibition was found after incubation for 48h, even when the concentration of HCDs was 400μgmL−1 (Fig. S22). The maximum concentration of HCDs used in drug delivery is <100μgmL−1, so the HCDs appear to be biocompatible. The cytotoxicity of DOX-HCDs was evaluated using four types of cells. As shown in Fig. S23, the cytotoxicity of both DOX-HCDs and free DOX gradually increased with increasing DOX concentration and incubation time. At the same DOX concentration, DOX-HCDs exhibited similar cytotoxicity to free DOX, indicating that the HCDs did not interfere with the pharmacodynamic activity of DOX. The above results suggest that these nontoxic HCDs are ideal drug delivery vehicles.

Luminescent HCDs were prepared as a delivery vehicle for DOX. The main advantages of HCDs as a drug delivery system are rapid uptake by cells, pH-controlled release of DOX and a lack of influence on drug activity. HCDs can also be used for cell imaging by exploiting their luminescence. HCDs show promise in bioimaging, biolabeling and biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Scientific Program-Nanoscience and Nanotechnology (No. 2011CB933600), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21175110), and the Natural Science Foundation Project of CQ CSTC (CSTC, 2010BB4004). We thank Prof. Huang Chengzhi, Prof. Huang Yuming, Dr. Liu Changhua and Dr. Tang Qian (Southwest University) for their help with experimental measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2013.03.009.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: This document file contain Supplementary figures and tables.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanoparticles as visible-light photocatalysts for efficient CO2 conversion and beyond
Light-emitting devices based on carbon dots’ electroluminescence
Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
